project__lead_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: West Hamilton Road and Riverbend Parkway
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School, Hamilton Elementary School, St. Mary’s Parish School

local_action_type.local_action_type_other: Development Agreement approval; Design Review for multifamily and commercial buildings.

development_type.transportation_type: On-site local streets, roundabout at West Hamilton Road/Riverbend Parkway, Class I multi-use trail, and transit stop with bus pull-outs and shelters.
development_type.development_type_other: Neighborhood park (5.2 acres), greenway and riparian restoration corridor (6.1 acres), and a small community center (4,500 sq ft).

project_issues_discussed_in_document.project_issues_other: Energy demand and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice considerations related to air quality and traffic exposure for nearby disadvantaged communities.

land_use__project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des:
The approximately 48.7-acre project site consists of three contiguous parcels located on the western edge of the City of Riverbend. The site is currently in agricultural use, primarily planted in irrigated alfalfa and seasonal row crops, with several remnant walnut trees along historic field boundaries. Existing improvements are limited to a small metal equipment shed, irrigation pumps, underground piping, and unpaved farm access drives. There are no existing residences on the site.

Current zoning: The property is zoned AG-20 (Agriculture, 20-acre minimum) within the City of Riverbend zoning code. This zoning district allows for agricultural production and accessory agricultural structures and does not currently permit urban-density residential or commercial uses.

General Plan designation: Under the City of Riverbend 2035 General Plan, the site is designated as Urban Reserve/Agriculture Transition (UR/AT), identifying it as a long-term growth area intended for eventual conversion from agriculture to a mix of residential and neighborhood-serving commercial uses once infrastructure and services are available. The proposed project includes a General Plan Amendment to change the designation to Mixed Use Neighborhood (MU-N), consistent with the City’s growth framework for this western growth area.

Surrounding land uses: To the north, the site is bounded by West Hamilton Road and existing single-family residential neighborhoods developed in the late 1990s, with lot sizes typically around 7,000 square feet. To the east, across an existing irrigation canal, is a small cluster of rural residential properties on 1–2 acre lots and a neighborhood park. To the south and west, the land remains in active agricultural production (primarily rice and alfalfa fields) within Colusa County’s jurisdiction. The Sacramento River and associated riparian corridor are located approximately 0.4 mile west of the project boundary. The site is relatively flat, with a gentle slope toward the southwest and a modest elevation difference of approximately 6–8 feet across the site.
land_use__project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project proposes to transform an approximately 48.7-acre agricultural site on the western edge of the City of Riverbend into a compact, walkable, mixed-use neighborhood that integrates housing, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, office space, and public open space. The project is intended to serve as a logical western extension of the existing city fabric and to implement the City’s long-range growth objectives for the Urban Reserve/Agriculture Transition area.

Key components of the proposed project are summarized below:

1. Residential Development
- Total units: 312 dwelling units.
- Product types:
  • Approximately 148 single-family detached units on lots ranging from 3,800 to 5,200 square feet, organized along a modified grid street pattern with alleys serving some rear-loaded garages.
  • Approximately 96 small-lot single-family or duet units (paired homes) designed to provide a more affordable ownership option while maintaining a neighborhood character.
  • Approximately 68 multifamily units in three-story garden-style buildings located closer to the mixed-use commercial core to support a more walkable environment and provide rental housing choices.
- Density and design: Overall residential density averages approximately 12.8 dwelling units per net residential acre, with higher densities near the commercial core and lower densities along the edges adjacent to existing single-family neighborhoods. The project includes front porches, sidewalks on both sides of most streets, street trees, and traffic-calming features such as narrow travel lanes and bulb-outs at key intersections.

2. Commercial and Office Development
- Neighborhood commercial: Approximately 68,500 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space is proposed, including a small-format grocery (roughly 25,000 sq ft), café and restaurant spaces, a pharmacy, personal service retail (such as a dry cleaner and hair salon), and small shops oriented around a central main street and plaza. Building heights would generally range from one to two stories, with active ground-floor frontages and pedestrian-oriented design.
- Office space: Approximately 45,000 square feet of professional and medical office space is proposed in two- and three-story buildings located at the northeast corner of the site near West Hamilton Road. Anticipated tenants include medical and dental offices, small professional firms (e.g., insurance, real estate, accounting), and potential coworking space. The office component is intended to provide local employment opportunities and reduce commute trips to larger regional centers.

3. Parks, Open Space, and Community Facilities
- Neighborhood park: A 5.2-acre public neighborhood park is proposed near the center of the project, with frontage on two public streets for visibility and access. Planned amenities include a lighted multi-purpose turf field, a children’s play area with shade structures, half-court basketball, a picnic grove with barbecues, restroom facilities, and a small amphitheater-style lawn that can accommodate community events and outdoor movie nights.
- Greenway and riparian corridor: Approximately 6.1 acres along the western edge of the site will be preserved and enhanced as a greenway and drainage corridor. This area will include native riparian plantings, bioswales designed to treat stormwater, and a Class I multi-use trail that connects to the city’s broader trail network. Interpretive signage will highlight local ecology, the agricultural history of the site, and the nearby Sacramento River.
- Community center: A 4,500-square-foot community building is proposed adjacent to the neighborhood park. The building is envisioned to include a large multi-purpose room, a small kitchenette, office space for homeowners’ association and community programming staff, and flexible meeting rooms that can be used for after-school programs, exercise classes, neighborhood association meetings, and cultural events.

4. Circulation and Transportation Improvements
- Internal street network: The project will develop a connected network of public and private streets, emphasizing walkability and connectivity to existing neighborhoods to the east and north. The main entrance will be from a new signalized intersection on West Hamilton Road, with a secondary access from Riverbend Parkway. Local streets will feature 10–11-foot travel lanes, on-street parking, landscaped parkways, and continuous sidewalks.
- Intersection improvements: The existing all-way stop at West Hamilton Road and Riverbend Parkway will be reconfigured as a modern single-lane roundabout to improve traffic flow and safety, particularly at peak hours and during school pick-up and drop-off periods. The project will contribute fair-share funding for off-site traffic signal timing upgrades and pedestrian crossing enhancements at nearby intersections serving Riverbend High School.
- Bicycle and pedestrian facilities: A Class I multi-use path will run along the western greenway and connect to on-street Class II bike lanes on West Hamilton Road. Sidewalks, mid-block pedestrian connections, and enhanced crosswalks with high-visibility striping and curb extensions will be provided to encourage walking and biking within and through the project.
- Transit accommodations: The project will include a new transit stop with bus pull-outs and shelters on West Hamilton Road, coordinated with the Colusa County Transit Agency. The stop will be located near the commercial main street and office buildings to facilitate convenient access for residents, employees, and visitors.

5. Utilities and Infrastructure
- Water supply: Potable water will be supplied by the City of Riverbend municipal water system. The project includes construction of new 12-inch and 8-inch water mains, fire hydrants, and individual service connections. A detailed water demand analysis indicates that the project’s projected average and peak demands can be accommodated by the City’s planned well and storage improvements, subject to implementation of water conservation measures such as high-efficiency fixtures and drought-tolerant landscaping.
- Wastewater: Sanitary sewer flows will be conveyed via new gravity sewer mains to an existing trunk line in Riverbend Parkway, which ultimately discharges to the City’s wastewater treatment plant. Capacity analysis completed for the Mitigated Negative Declaration confirms that the system has sufficient capacity with minor off-site upsizing of an 8-inch segment to 10 inches. The project will construct on-site lift stations only if final design indicates gravity flow is not feasible to reach the existing trunk.
- Storm drainage: The project will use a combination of on-site detention basins, green infrastructure (bioswales, rain gardens, and permeable pavement in selected parking areas), and underground piping to manage and treat stormwater runoff. The drainage system is designed to maintain post-project peak flows at or below existing conditions for the 10- and 100-year storm events and to improve water quality through filtration and infiltration prior to discharge into Stone Slough.
- Dry utilities: Electric, natural gas, telecommunications, and broadband will be extended to the site via underground conduits. The project will work with the local utility provider to incorporate EV charging infrastructure in commercial parking areas and to ensure adequate capacity for electric heat pump-based building systems should future tenants elect to pursue all-electric designs.

6. Entitlements and Approvals
The project requires several discretionary approvals from the City of Riverbend, including: (1) a General Plan Amendment from Urban Reserve/Agriculture Transition (UR/AT) to Mixed Use Neighborhood (MU-N); (2) a Specific Plan adoption for the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village, establishing land use allocations, development standards, and design guidelines; (3) a Rezone from AG-20 to a combination of MU-N, R-2 (Medium Density Residential), and C-N (Neighborhood Commercial); (4) Tentative Subdivision Map(s) for creation of residential, commercial, and open space lots and public rights-of-way; (5) Planned Unit Development (PUD) approval to allow flexibility in lot sizes, setbacks, and building types while achieving superior urban design; (6) Site Plan and Design Review for the multifamily, commercial, and office buildings; (7) a Conditional Use Permit for the community center and certain commercial uses such as a drive-through pharmacy; and (8) approval of a Development Agreement outlining phasing, infrastructure financing, and public benefit commitments.

7. Phasing
The project is anticipated to be built in three major phases over approximately 8–10 years, depending on market conditions:
- Phase 1 (Years 1–3): Construction of backbone infrastructure (major streets, water, sewer, drainage), the initial 120 single-family units, the neighborhood park, and a portion of the greenway trail, as well as the transit stop and one of the smaller commercial pads (e.g., café and neighborhood services).
- Phase 2 (Years 3–6): Development of the remaining single-family and duet units, completion of the community center building, and construction of approximately half of the office space and additional commercial buildings along the main street.
- Phase 3 (Years 6–10): Completion of the multifamily buildings, remaining commercial and office space, final buildout of the greenway and trail system, and any remaining intersection or off-site improvements identified in the traffic analysis.

8. Environmental Commitments
As part of the Mitigated Negative Declaration, the project proponent has agreed to implement a comprehensive set of mitigation measures addressing biological resources (e.g., pre-construction nesting bird surveys, riparian habitat enhancement, and compensatory mitigation for any impacts to seasonal wetlands), cultural resources (e.g., worker training, monitoring during ground disturbance in sensitive areas, and procedures for inadvertent discovery of cultural materials or human remains), air quality and greenhouse gas emissions (e.g., use of low-VOC paints, EV charging infrastructure, and compliance with applicable construction emission control standards), noise (e.g., construction hour limitations and building design features to reduce interior noise levels), and traffic (e.g., fair-share contributions to off-site improvements and implementation of a transportation demand management program for office tenants).

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is designed to create a cohesive, pedestrian-friendly neighborhood that provides a range of housing types, local jobs, and amenities while respecting adjacent agricultural lands and sensitive environmental resources. The project’s mix of uses, open space framework, and transportation improvements are intended to support a more sustainable pattern of growth for the City of Riverbend, reduce vehicle miles traveled relative to conventional suburban development, and offer residents a high quality of life with convenient access to daily needs, recreation, and community gathering spaces.
